topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The quarterly bonus meeting felt like a slow descent into a pit of unease. I watched Sarah, the newest analyst, receive her payout, and a strange tightness coiled in my chest. Her smile was bright, almost too practiced, as she accepted the larger sum. I cross-referenced the departmental salary bands in my head, a familiar, nagging doubt beginning to surface. It simply didn't align with the hours she logged or the complexity of the projects she handled. I cleared my throat, trying to sound casual as I asked my manager for clarification on the compensation structure for her role. The silence that followed was thick, heavy with unspoken answers.

[story 2]

I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before my eyes. The figures for Mark, the junior developer, were undeniably higher than mine, even after factoring in overtime. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, making my hands clammy on the keyboard. I leaned back in my chair, deliberately taking a long sip of cold coffee, trying to keep my expression neutral. I decided to just observe. I’d wait for something concrete, some undeniable proof that explained the disparity, rather than jumping to conclusions based on a gut feeling. The air in the open-plan office suddenly felt thin.

[story 3]

When the HR notification landed in my inbox, I felt a distinct flutter of apprehension. It was about a salary adjustment for a colleague I barely knew, someone who seemed to glide through the corporate structure effortlessly. I reread the email three times, scrutinizing every percentage point. My jaw felt tight. I walked over to the water cooler, pretending to adjust my water bottle, my gaze fixed on the reflection of my own strained features in the polished chrome. I needed more data, more context, before I allowed myself to process this discrepancy.
